artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Kids are finally in bed, so I’m catching up on life and McKinley all at once.

I keep thinking about how much of this project is tangled up with our own weekends. Those regular Saturday mornings from 9–11 a.m. with Ana and Mateo at McKinley feel so different now that I know the exact 12,500 square feet of equipment and surfacing we’re tearing out. Every time we walk past the north play structure, I still see Ana’s fall on March 18, 2023 in my head, and then that March 2023 ER visit at UC Davis Children’s when they called it a “mild concussion” but I sat there feeling like the worst planner-mom in Sacramento.

Then there’s Mateo. Between his June 2022 autism diagnosis at the UC Davis MIND Institute and Dr. Patel’s written recommendation about needing a fully accessible, lower-stimulation play area close to home, it’s impossible for me to pretend this is just another Class 1/3/4 park upgrade. And after his eval at Kaiser Point West and our May 7, 2023 Southside Park walkthrough with you and Priya, I can’t un-see how much difference the poured-in-place rubber, transfer platforms, and accessible swings make for kids like him.

I’m still irritated about that October 17, 2023 complaint letter from Daniel Ruiz accusing me of “fast-tracking” McKinley for my own kids and ignoring noise and traffic. Meanwhile, he has no idea how hard I worked to keep this squarely within CEQA Guidelines Sections 15301, 15303, and 15304 so we don’t lose another 12–18 months to a full EIR while Ana and Mateo keep dodging exposed footings.

Anyway, enough venting. Are you still free to grab tacos after Ana’s soccer practice Tuesday? I could use something crunchy and salty that isn’t a CEQA memo.

– L

Personal reflection – McKinley + family

I need to keep reminding myself why this playground project matters beyond the technical language I’ve been living in.

1) Ana’s fall – March 18, 2023, north play structure at McKinley Park. Split rubber tiles, weird lip at the edge. She went down hard, and we ended up at UC Davis Children’s Hospital with that “mild concussion” diagnosis. I still see her in the pediatric ER with the tiny bracelet on. That’s when I started sketching how to remove and replace that exact structure and the surrounding surfacing.

2) Mateo – June 2022 autism diagnosis at the UC Davis MIND Institute. Dr. Patel’s OT report spelled it out: he needs a fully accessible, predictable, lower-noise play space close to home. Add in the pediatric OT visit with Dr. Elena Ruiz at Sutter Children’s Center in mid‑April 2023, when she pointed out the lack of compliant transfer platforms and surfacing for kids with emerging motor delays. Her comments about poured‑in‑place rubber and inclusive swings are literally built into the design.

3) Mom – I keep flashing back to walking McKinley with her for my UC Davis MURP capstone in Spring 2012, after her 2010 knee surgery at St. Joseph’s Medical Center in Stockton. She was leaning on her cane, struggling with the old slopes. That fieldwork is the reason I can read a grading plan in my sleep now.

Note to self: these personal threads stay in my head and my journal, not in public documents. But they’re why I fight for every accessible route and every inch of rubberized surfacing.

Hola Mami,

I wanted to write instead of just sending quick texts, because there’s a lot swirling around in my head.

First, I keep thinking about you and Papá and that awful 2019 Stockton code enforcement case with the little rental. It still makes me angry that the City of Stockton pushed you into that expensive environmental review for the unpermitted work when it was such a small project. Watching you drain part of your retirement savings to pay for studies you didn’t even understand is one of the reasons that, since I moved into the Senior Planner role in 2020, I’ve been so stubborn about using CEQA categorical exemptions correctly for small, low‑impact projects.

That’s exactly what I’m doing now with the McKinley Park Playground Accessibility and Safety Upgrade Project. It’s just replacing about 12,500 square feet of aging equipment and non‑compliant surfacing inside an existing playground, plus minor path and fencing work. No expansion, no new parking. I’ve structured it firmly under the Class 1, 3, and 4 exemptions so families aren’t blindsided by unnecessary studies and fees the way you and Papá were.

I’m also still feeling the sting from that May 5, 2022 internal audit by the City Auditor’s Office that said our division wasted $47,000 on full CEQA documents for little park projects that could have used exemptions. Some of that landed on my 2023 performance review, so you can imagine why I’m double‑checking every line now.

On a lighter note, the kids and I are planning to drive down I‑5 next Sunday to see you. Ana wants to show you her new Spanish book, and Mateo says he’s going to “fix” your TV remote again.

Te quiero mucho,
Laura

Carlos: Hey sis, you still coming to Elk Grove this Saturday? We’re doing carne asada if the baby lets us sleep Friday night lol.

Laura: Yes! I’ll bring tortillas from the co‑op. Might be a little late because Ana has soccer at McKinley in the morning.

Carlos: You still taking them to that playground with the broken fence??

Laura: For now, but that’s exactly what I’m fixing. Remember I told you about December 2, 2022, when that CPS social worker at the McKinley Park community center pulled me aside and asked if I had a “safety plan” for Ana and Mateo because of the busted perimeter fencing and exposed footings near the 5–12 play structure? I felt so judged.

Laura: That conversation is why the new project includes full perimeter fencing, safety signage, and reconfiguring the accessible route from the community center to the playground. I never want to have to justify to a mandated reporter again why my kids are playing near obvious hazards.

Carlos: Ugh, people. But good you’re fixing it.

Laura: And I have to be extra careful. On October 17, 2023 this neighbor, Daniel Ruiz, sent a formal complaint to the City saying I was “fast‑tracking” the McKinley project to benefit my own kids and ignoring noise and traffic. So now I’m triple‑documenting that it fits under CEQA Guidelines Sections 15301, 15303, and 15304 – no expansion of the playground footprint, no new parking.

Carlos: They picked the wrong hermana to mess with.

Laura: Exactly. Anyway, what time should we come Saturday? I’ll bring guac too.

Work journal – tying threads together

Today I biked past McKinley on my way from 2417 H Street, Apt 3 over to the office at 300 Richards Boulevard, 3rd Floor, and it hit me how long this has been in my life.

Spring 2012: UC Davis MURP capstone. I modeled ADA‑compliant routes and inclusive play pods for McKinley Park with Mom (Isabel) as my unofficial test subject after her 2010 knee surgery at St. Joseph’s Medical Center in Stockton. We walked those slopes together, her with the cane, me scribbling notes.

Fast‑forward to being Senior Planner. I could have tried to build the full capstone vision – the big plaza and shade structure – into the current McKinley Park Playground Accessibility and Safety Upgrade Project. Instead I deliberately scaled it back to the essentials: removing and replacing ~12,500 square feet of deteriorated play equipment and non‑compliant wood fiber surfacing, fixing the worst path slopes, adding inclusive play elements (transfer platforms, ground‑level sensory panels, accessible swings), new poured‑in‑place rubberized safety surfacing, upgraded accessible pathways to the parking lot, restrooms, and community center, plus perimeter fencing, safety signage, and minor turf/landscape restoration.

I left the plaza and big shade structure out because they’d likely push us into full CEQA review instead of the clean Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), and Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land) categorical exemptions. After the May 5, 2022 City Auditor’s Office report and the $47,000 consultant overspend that showed up on my 2023 performance review, I’m not eager to repeat history.

Note: keep Mom’s medical history, Ana’s fall, and Mateo’s diagnoses out of anything public-facing. Those are my internal compass, not the official story.

Hi Priya,

Hope your week isn’t completely swallowed by grading plans.

I was thinking about our May 7, 2023 meetup at Southside Park with Daniel – the one right after Mateo’s evaluation at Kaiser Permanente Point West – and how much that informal walk has shaped the McKinley Park Playground Accessibility and Safety Upgrade Project. The way you broke down the details of the poured‑in‑place rubberized surfacing, transfer platforms, and accessible swings we saw there made it easy for me to translate them into specs.

McKinley’s scope is now basically: remove and replace about 12,500 square feet of aging play equipment and non‑compliant surfacing, regrade the worst ADA issues on the paths connecting to the parking lot, restrooms, and community center, and add inclusive play elements for ages 2–5 and 5–12 – transfer platforms, ground‑level sensory panels, accessible swings, etc. We’re also installing new perimeter fencing and safety signage and doing minor turf/landscape restoration where construction disturbs things.

On the CEQA side, I’m keeping it firmly under the Class 1, 3, and 4 categorical exemptions – Sections 15301, 15303, and 15304 – so we don’t end up wasting money the way our division did before that May 5, 2022 City Auditor’s Office audit that flagged $47,000 in unnecessary consultant fees. I still wince thinking about how that ended up in my 2023 performance review.

Anyway, if you’re up for it, I’d love a quick sanity check on the draft layout – just an extra set of eyes on the ADA routes and any potential trip hazards around the inclusive equipment. Coffee at Old Soul next week? I can bring printouts and buy you a latte.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: McKinley playground details + quick ADA gut check?

artifacts.content:
Event: File Notice of Exemption – McKinley Park Playground Accessibility and Safety Upgrade

Date: Thursday, January 22, 2026
Time: 9:30 a.m. – 11:00 a.m.
Location: Start at City of Sacramento Community Development Department, Planning Division (300 Richards Boulevard, 3rd Floor), then walk forms over to:
– Sacramento County Clerk
– State Office of Planning and Research (OPR)

Attendees: Laura M. Hernandez (lead), internal courier (TBD)

Notes:
– Double‑check that the project title is exactly “McKinley Park Playground Accessibility and Safety Upgrade Project.”
– Confirm the project location language: McKinley Park, 601 Alhambra Blvd, Sacramento, CA 95816; APNs 007‑0223‑004 and 007‑0223‑005; City of Sacramento, Sacramento County.
– Re‑skim exemption language before walking over: Class 1 (Existing Facilities), Section 15301; Class 3 (New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures), Section 15303; Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land), Section 15304. Make sure the “no expansion of use, no increase in park capacity, all work confined to already‑developed recreational area” phrasing is consistent.
– Remember why this matters: Ana’s March 2023 concussion from the fall at McKinley, Mateo’s June 2022 autism diagnosis at the UC Davis MIND Institute and Dr. Patel’s recommendation for accessible, calmer play, plus Mom and Papá’s 2019 Stockton rental nightmare and the May 5, 2022 audit about $47,000 in unnecessary CEQA consultant costs. All the reasons to get this right and keep it moving.
– Signature block: “Laura M. Hernandez, Senior Planner.” Date line should read 1/22/2026. Confirm box checked for “Signed by Lead Agency.”

metadata.title: File McKinley NOE – County Clerk + OPR
